title: Is Cuba Next?
author: Dana Sherne
text:

Is Cuba Next?

Live On June 30, 2026  |  1:00pm ET

After the United States ousted Venezuela’s Nicolás Maduro in January and took out Iran’s senior leadership in February, U.S. President Donald Trump set his sights on Cuba, claiming that he would have the “honor of taking” the island. Now, after five months of a U.S. oil blockade, Havana is restructuring its economy and even publicly welcoming diplomacy with Washington. 
So, is a new form of regime change underway in Cuba, or will the Trump administration seek to replicate the model of Venezuela or Iran? Michael Bustamante, a leading historian of Cuban-American relations, will join FP Live to assess the White House’s options.

Host and Guests

Michael Bustamante

Cuban and Cuban-American studies chair, University of Miami

Michael Bustamante is an associate professor of history and the Emilio Bacardí Moreau chair and program director of Cuban and Cuban-American studies at the University of Miami. He is the author of Cuban Memory Wars: Retrospective Politics in Revolution and Exile and a co-editor of the journal Cuban Studies. Bustamante also serves as the director of academic programs at the Cuban Heritage Collection.

Michael Bustamante is an associate professor of history and the Emilio Bacardí Moreau chair and program director of Cuban and Cuban-American studies at the University of Miami. He is the author of Cuban Memory Wars: Retrospective Politics in Revolution and Exile and a co-editor of the journal Cuban Studies. Bustamante also serves as the director of academic programs at the Cuban Heritage Collection.

Ravi Agrawal

Editor in chief, Foreign Policy

Ravi Agrawal is the editor in chief of Foreign Policy and host of FP Live. Before joining FP in 2018, Agrawal worked at CNN for more than a decade, including as New Delhi bureau chief and correspondent. He is the author of India Connected: How the Smartphone Is Transforming the World’s Largest Democracy.

On-Demand from FP Live

What SpaceX Means for the World

June 25, 2026

SpaceX’s successful listing on Nasdaq (briefly) turned CEO Elon Musk into the world’s first trillionaire—and the most powerful person deciding the planet’s role in space. What does our new era of space capitalism mean for the role of states? And specifically in Musk’s case, does one man have too much power? Historian Quinn Slobodian, co-author of the new book Muskism, argues that Musk has designs on controlling discourse on Earth—powered by technology in space. He joined FP Live to discuss how countries and companies can navigate Musk’s dominance.

Deciphering the U.S.-Iran Deal

June 23, 2026

A U.S.-Iran deal to open the Strait of Hormuz was inked last week, but many key details remain undecided. How will the two sides handle the nuclear issue? Who are the real winners and losers from the conflict? FP’s Ravi Agrawal sat down with Ali Vaez, who has been speaking with key players on both sides of the negotiations. Vaez is the Iran project director at the International Crisis Group.

Is the U.S.-Israel Special Relationship Over?

June 16, 2026

Polls show that, for the first time, Americans view Palestinians more sympathetically than they do Israelis. Prominent U.S. lawmakers are openly criticizing the country’s bipartisan policies toward Israel. In an essay for FP’s Summer print issue, Joshua Leifer argues that the two countries’ cooperation in the Iran war may represent the alliance’s zenith—and a true distancing may follow. So, is the U.S.-Israel special relationship over? Leifer, a columnist for Haaretz, joined FP Live to explore trends in the U.S.-Israeli partnership and how that could disrupt politics in both countries.

The Geopolitics of Soccer

June 9, 2026

This year’s FIFA World Cup, the largest in the competition’s history, comes amid a particularly fraught geopolitical backdrop. Not only is a host country at war with one of the participating nations, but the three host nations have spent the last year sparring over immigration, trade, and even national sovereignty. What does this tournament tell us about the state of the world? FP’s Ravi Agrawal asked Mehreen Khan, a former sports journalist and now the economics editor of The Times.
